      When a function makes a recursive call, it:

      1. Pauses the current function and stores its execution context in a stack.
      2. Executes the nested function call.
      3. Retrieves the old execution context from the stack once the nested function call has completed.
      4. Resumes the outer function from where it left off.

      Let’s see what happens during the pow(2, 3) call.

      1. The pow() function is called with x equal to 2 and n equal to 3. Its execution context is stored in the execution context stack.
      2. It makes recursive calls to itself with decreasing values of n until n is equal to 1. The execution contexts of the nested function calls are stored in the stack.
      3. It returns x when n is equal to 1.
      4. The return values are multiplied together to get the final result. The old execution contexts are retrieved from the stack and the outer function is resumed from where it left off. The final result of the pow() function is 8.

      Recursive traversals

      function preOrderTraversal(node) {
          if (node == null) {
              return;
          }

          console.log(node.value);
          preOrderTraversal(node.left);
          preOrderTraversal(node.right);
      }  
      const tree = {
          value: 1,
          left: {
            value: 2,
            left: {
              value: 4,
              left: null,
              right: null
            },
            right: {
              value: 5,
              left: null,
              right: null
            }
          },
          right: {
            value: 3,
            left: {
              value: 6,
              left: null,
              right: null
            },
            right: {
              value: 7,
              left: null,
              right: null
            }
          }
        };
        preOrderTraversal(tree);

      The preOrderTraversal() function performs a pre-order traversal of a binary tree as follows:

      1. It takes a node as an argument and checks if it is null. If it is, the function returns without doing anything.
      2. It outputs the value of the node to the console.
      3. It calls itself with the left child of the node as an argument.
      4. It calls itself with the right child of the node as an argument.
      5. The function returns after the left and right sub-trees have been traversed.
  • Rest parameters and spread syntax

    • Rest parameters ...

      The rest of the parameters can be included in the function definition by using three dots ... followed by the name of the array that will contain them. The dots literally mean “gather the remaining parameters into an array”.For instance, to gather all arguments into array args:

      function sumAll(...args) { // args is the name for the array
          let sum = 0;

          for (let arg of args) sum += arg;

          return sum;
      }
      console.log(sumAll(1)); // 1
      console.log(sumAll(1, 2)); // 3
      console.log(sumAll(1, 2, 3)); // 6

      Here the first two arguments go into variables and the rest go into titles array:

      function showName(firstName, lastName, ...titles) {
          console.log(firstName + ' ' + lastName); // Julius Caesar

          // the rest go into titles array
          // i.e. titles = ["Consul", "Imperator"]
          console.log(titles[0]); // Consul
          console.log(titles[1]); // Imperator
          console.log(titles.length); // 2
      }
      showName("Julius", "Caesar", "Consul", "Imperator");

      The rest parameters must be at the end

      The “arguments” variable

      There is also a special array-like object named arguments that contains all arguments by their index.

      function showName() {
          console.log( arguments.length );
          console.log( arguments[0] );
          console.log( arguments[1] );
       
          // it's iterable
          // for(let arg of arguments) alert(arg);
        }
       
        // shows: 2, Julius, Caesar
        showName("Julius", "Caesar");
       
        // shows: 1, Ilya, undefined (no second argument)
        showName("Ilya");

      it's for legacy code , rest parameters are preferred.

      Arrow functions do not have "arguments"

    • Spread syntax

      The spread syntax in JavaScript allows you to expand an iterable (such as an array) into individual elements. It is represented by three dots (...).

      //In this example, the copy array will contain the same elements as the numbers array
      const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4];
      const copy = [...numbers];

      // You can also use the spread syntax to merge multiple arrays into a single array:
      const a = [1, 2, 3];
      const b = [4, 5, 6];
      const c = [...a, ...b];

      // The spread syntax can also be used to expand an iterable in function calls:
      function sum(x, y, z) {
          return x + y + z;
        }
        const numberss = [1, 2, 3];
        console.log(sum(...numbers)); // 6


      // Split the word into an array of individual characters.
      const word = 'hello';
      const chars = [...word];

      // Output the resulting array.
      console.log(chars); // ['h', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o']

      // Define a function that takes individual letters as arguments.
      function printLetters(a, b, c, d, e) {
        // Output the letters to the console.
        console.log(a, b, c, d, e);
      }

      // Call the function with the spread syntax to expand the word into separate arguments.
      printLetters(...word); // h e l l o

      with objects

      // Define an object with some properties.
      const obj = { x: 1, y: 2, z: 3 };

      // Use the spread syntax to create a copy of the object.
      const copy = { ...obj };

      // Output the copy to the console.
      console.log(copy); // { x: 1, y: 2, z: 3 }

      // Define two more objects with overlapping properties.
      const a = { x: 1, y: 2 };
      const b = { y: 3, z: 4 };

      // Use the spread syntax to merge the two objects into a single object.
      const c = { ...a, ...b };

      // Output the merged object to the console.
      console.log(c); // { x: 1, y: 3, z: 4 }
  • Variable scope, closure

    • Variable scope, closure

      1. JavaScript is a language that places a lot of emphasis on functions.
      2. Functions can be created at any time and passed as arguments to other functions.
      3. Functions can access variables that are outside of them (known as "outer" variables).
      4. If an outer variable changes after a function is created, the function will still have access to the original value.
      5. When a function is passed as an argument and called from another location, it will have access to the outer variables in that new location.

      Code blocks

      Keyword Scope Reassignable?
      var Function Yes
      let Block Yes
      const Block No

      // Variables declared with the "let" keyword are block-scoped, which means that they are only visible within the block in which they are declared.
      {
          let message = "Hello";  // Declare a block-scoped variable "message" with the value "Hello"
          alert(message);  // Output: "Hello"
      }

      alert(message);  // This line will throw an error because the "message" variable is not defined outside of the block

      // We can use blocks to isolate pieces of code and prevent variables from leaking into the global scope or into other blocks
      {
          let message = "Hello";
          alert(message);  // Output: "Hello"
      }

      {
          let message = "Goodbye";
          alert(message);  // Output: "Goodbye"
      }

      // Without separate blocks, we would get an error if we tried to declare a variable with the same name as an existing variable
      let message = "Hello";
      alert(message);  // Output: "Hello"

      let message = "Goodbye";  // This line will throw an error because the "message" variable has already been declared
      alert(message);

      // Variables declared within a "for" loop or an "if" statement are also only visible within the block of that loop or statement
      if (true) {
          let phrase = "Hello!";  // Declare a block-scoped variable "phrase" with the value "Hello!"
          alert(phrase);  // Output: "Hello!"
      }

      alert(phrase);  // This line will throw an error because the "phrase" variable is not defined outside of the block

      for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
          // The "i" variable is only visible within this "for" loop
          alert(i);  // Output: 0, 1, 2
      }

      alert(i);  // This line will throw an error because the "i" variable is not defined outside of the loop

    • Nested functions


      // This is the outer function "sayHiBye"
      function sayHiBye(firstName, lastName) {

          // This is the nested function "getFullName"
          function getFullName() {
              // The nested function has access to the variables declared in the outer function
              return firstName + " " + lastName;
          }

          // We can use the nested function to return the full name
          alert("Hello, " + getFullName());
          alert("Bye, " + getFullName());

      }

      // We can call the outer function to run the nested function
      sayHiBye("John", "Doe");  // Output: "Hello, John Doe" and "Bye, John Doe"

    • Lexical Environment

      click here

    • Closure

      function outer() {
          let x = 10;

          function inner() {
              console.log(x);  // Output: 10
          }

          return inner;
      }

      let closure = outer();
      closure();  

      in this code, the outer function declares a variable x with the let keyword and assigns it a value of 10. It then defines an inner function called inner that logs the value of x. Finally, it returns the inner function. When the outer function is called, it creates a new lexical environment and associates it with the inner function using the [[Environment]] property. This lexical environment contains the identifier-variable mapping for the x variable, which has a value of 10. When the closure function is called, it uses the [[Environment]] property to access the lexical environment associated with the inner function and retrieves the value of the x variable. Because the x variable has a value of 10, the closure function logs the value 10. I hope this helps to clarify the role of the [[Environment]] property in JavaScript! Let me know if you have any other questions.

    • The [[Environment]] property is a hidden internal property of functions in JavaScript that references the lexical environment in which the function was created. It is used by closures to remember and access the outer variables that they need to close over. Every time you create a function in JavaScript, the JavaScript engine creates a new lexical environment and associates it with the function using the [[Environment]] property. This lexical environment contains the identifier-variable mappings for the code that the function executes.

    • Garbage collection

      click here

  • the old "var"

    • “var” has no block scope

      Variables, declared with var, are either function-scoped or global-scoped. They are visible through blocks.

      if (true) {
          var test = true; // use "var" instead of "let"
      }
      console.log(test); // true, the variable lives after if

      The same thing for loops: var cannot be block- or loop-local:

      for (var i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
          var one = 1;
          // ...
      }
      console.log(i);   // 10, "i" is visible after loop, it's a global variable
      console.log(one); // 1, "one" is visible after loop, it's a global variable

      If a code block is inside a function, then var becomes a function-level variable:

      function sayHi() {
          if (true) {
              var phrase = "Hello";
          }

          console.log(phrase); // works
      }

      sayHi();
      console.log(phrase); // ReferenceError: phrase is not defined

    • “var” tolerates redeclarations

      With var, we can redeclare a variable any number of times. If we use var with an already-declared variable, it’s just ignored:

      var user = "Pete";
      var user = "John"; // this "var" does nothing (already declared)
      // ...it doesn't trigger an error

      console.log(user); // John

    • “var” variables can be declared below their use

      function sayHi() {
          phrase = "Hello";

          console.log(phrase);

          var phrase;
      }
      sayHi();

      Declarations are hoisted, but assignments are not.

      function sayHi() {
          console.log(phrase);

          var phrase = "Hello";
      }
      sayHi();

    • IIFE:“immediately-invoked function expressions”

      • // Ways to create IIFE

      (function () {
          console.log("Parentheses around the function");
      })();

      (function () {
          console.log("Parentheses around the whole thing");
      }());

      !function () {
          console.log("Bitwise NOT operator starts the expression");
      }();

      +function () {
          console.log("Unary plus starts the expression");
      }();
  • Global object

    • global object

      • The global object provides variables and functions that are available anywhere in the environment.
      • In a browser, the global object is called window. In Node.js, it is called global.
      • The standardized name for the global object is globalThis, which is supported in all major browsers.
      • It is recommended to use globalThis instead of window in scripts that may run in other environments.

      All properties of the global object can be accessed directly

      console.log("Hello");
      // is the same as
      window.console.log("Hello");

      In a browser, global functions and variables declared with var (not let/const!) become the property of the global object:

      var gVar = 5;
      console.log(window.gVar); // 5 (became a property of the global object)

    • function declaration

      // This function declaration creates a global variable "gFunc"
      function gFunc() {
          // Function code goes here
      }
      console.log(window.gFunc);  // Output: function gFunc() { ... }
      // Declare a variable with the "let" keyword
      let gLet = 5;
      console.log(window.gLet);  // Output: undefined (does not become a property of the global object)  

      In this example, the function declaration function gFunc() { ... } creates a global variable gFunc, which becomes a property of the global object (e.g. window.gFunc). On the other hand, the let declaration let gLet = 5 does not create a global variable. Instead, it is only accessible within the block in which it is defined. This can be seen in the second console.log statement, which logs undefined to the console because gLet is not a property of the global object.

    • Using for polyfills

      We use the global object to test for support of modern language features.

      For instance, test if a built-in Promise object exists (it doesn’t in really old browsers):

      if (!window.Promise) {
          console.log("Your browser is really old!");
      }

      If there’s none (say, we’re in an old browser), we can create “polyfills”: add functions that are not supported by the environment, but exist in the modern standard.

      if (!window.Promise) {
          window.Promise = ... // custom implementation of the modern language feature
      }
  • Function object, NFE

    • Function object, NFE

      In JavaScript, functions are objects.

      A good way to imagine functions is as callable “action objects”. We can not only call them, but also treat them as objects: add/remove properties, pass by reference etc.

    • The “name” property

      Function objects contain some useable properties.For instance, a function’s name is accessible as the “name” property:

      function sayHi() {
          console.log("Hi");
      }
      console.log(sayHi.name); // sayHi

      What’s kind of funny, the name-assigning logic is smart. It also assigns the correct name to a function even if it’s created without one, and then immediately assigned:

      let sayHi = function () {
          console.log("Hi");
      };
      console.log(sayHi.name); // sayHi (there's a name!)

      Object methods have names too:

      let user = {

          sayHi() {
              // ...
          },

          sayBye: function () {
              // ...
          }

      }
      console.log(user.sayHi.name); // sayHi
      console.log(user.sayBye.name); // sayBye

      There are cases when there’s no way to figure out the right name. In that case, the name property is empty, like here:

      // function created inside array
      let arr = [function () { }];

      console.log(arr[0].name); // <empty string>
      // the engine has no way to set up the right name, so there is none

    • The “length” property

      There is another built-in property “length” that returns the number of function parameters, for instance:

      function f1(a) { }
      function f2(a, b) { }
      function many(a, b, ...more) { }
      console.log(f1.length); // 1
      console.log(f2.length); // 2
      console.log(many.length); // 2

      we can use the length as follow

      function ask(question, ...handlers) {
          // Prompt the user with the specified question and store the result in a variable
          let isYes = confirm(question);

          // Iterate through each handler function in the "handlers" array
          for (let handler of handlers) {
              // Check if the current handler function has a length of 0 (meaning it takes no arguments)
              if (handler.length == 0) {
                  // If the handler function takes no arguments, call it if the user answered "yes" to the question
                  if (isYes) handler();
              } else {
                  // If the handler function takes at least one argument, call it and pass in the value of "isYes" as an argument
                  handler(isYes);
              }
          }

      }
      // Call the "ask" function with two handler functions as arguments
      // For a positive answer, both handler functions will be called
      // For a negative answer, only the second handler function will be called
      ask("Question?", () => console.log('You said yes'), result => console.log(result));  

    • Custom properties

      We can also add properties of our own.

      Here we add the counter property to track the total calls count:

      function sayHi() {
          console.log("Hi");

          // let's count how many times we run
          sayHi.counter++;
      }
      sayHi.counter = 0; // initial value

      sayHi(); // Hi
      sayHi(); // Hi
      console.log(`Called ${sayHi.counter} times`); // Called 2 times

      Function properties can replace closures sometimes. For instance, we can rewrite the counter function example from the chapter Variable scope, closure to use a function property:

      function makeCounter() {
          // instead of:
          // let count = 0
          function counter() {
              return counter.count++;
          };

          counter.count = 0;

          return counter;
      }
      let counter = makeCounter();
      console.log(counter()); // 0
      console.log(counter()); // 1
  • The "new Function" syntax
  • Scheduling: setTimeout and setInterval
  • Decorators and forwarding, call/apply
  • Function binding
  • Arrow functions revisited
